fact: Athens crammed its population behind its walls during a Spartan siege when the plague broke out in 430 BC
source: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 2, Chapters 47-54

fact: Pericles ordered the countryside evacuated, bringing rural populations inside the city walls
source: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 2, Chapter 14

fact: Refugees sheltered in makeshift conditions between the Long Walls connecting Athens to Piraeus
source: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 2, Chapter 17

fact: The sickness started in the head (eyes, throat) and progressed through the entire body organ by organ
source: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 2, Chapter 49 — Thucydides' firsthand clinical description of symptoms

fact: Approximately a quarter of the Athenian population died in the plague
source: Widely documented estimate; Thucydides reports massive losses including 4,400 hoplites and 300 cavalry (Book 3, Ch. 87). Modern historians estimate 25% of the population, roughly 75,000–100,000 deaths.
